algorithms.title: Choking (FBAO) — Responsive Victim (2025)
algorithms.description: Management of a responsive victim with a foreign-body airway obstruction, by age and body type.
steps: Assess severity: if the victim can cough forcefully, speak, or breathe (mild), encourage coughing and do not interfere.
If severe (cannot speak, cough effectively, or breathe) and responsive, act immediately.
Adult or child: deliver 5 back blows between the shoulder blades, then 5 abdominal thrusts; alternate these cycles.
Pregnant or obese adult: replace abdominal thrusts with chest thrusts (still alternating with back blows).
Infant: alternate 5 back blows (face-down) with 5 chest thrusts (face-up), supporting the head and neck; never use abdominal thrusts.
Continue until the object is expelled or the victim becomes unresponsive.

algorithms.title: Choking Victim Who Becomes Unresponsive
algorithms.description: Transition from choking relief to CPR when the victim loses responsiveness.
steps: Carefully lower the victim to the ground.
Activate the emergency response system (or ensure it has been activated).
Begin CPR starting with chest compressions.
Each time you open the airway to give breaths, look inside the mouth.
Remove the foreign object only if you can clearly see it — do not perform blind finger sweeps.
Continue CPR; the compressions themselves can help expel the obstruction, and continue until relief, ROSC, or handoff.
